title: The Place That Made Me Want to Build Something Here
subtitle: Some places you visit once. Some places quietly become part of your future.
shortDesc: A peaceful valley where locals feel like family and life moves slower than anywhere else.
founderNote: I didn’t just travel here — I connected with this place. The people, the pace, the simplicity… it made me think that one day, I want to build a small homestay here and be part of this life.
story: Jibhi wasn’t just another trip for me. It felt different from the moment I arrived — not because of the views, but because of the people.
The locals here are some of the sweetest I’ve met. No rush, no attitude, just simple conversations and genuine warmth.
I stayed between Jibhi and Shoja, exploring both sides. Jibhi is calm, but Shoja feels even deeper and untouched.
Most days had no plan — just walking, sitting near the river, or talking to locals. Those simple days felt the most complete.
At one point, I realized — this isn’t just a place I want to visit again. It’s a place I want to build something in.

days.title: Delhi → Aut → Jibhi
days.content: Overnight bus from Delhi to Aut. From Aut, took a local cab/shared vehicle to Jibhi (1.5–2 hrs). Reached by afternoon, checked into homestay, and spent evening near the river.

days.title: Explore Jibhi & local cafes
days.content: Slow morning. Walked around Jibhi village, visited small cafés, explored wooden bridges and nearby forest trails. No fixed plan — just exploring freely.

days.title: Shoja & Jalori Pass
days.content: Took a ride to Shoja (30–40 mins). Continued to Jalori Pass (10,800 ft). Cold, windy, and peaceful. Spent time there and returned by evening.

days.title: Tirthan Valley exploration
days.content: Visited Tirthan Valley side — riverside walks, small villages, and peaceful spots. Less touristy, more raw nature.

days.title: Return — Jibhi → Aut → Delhi
days.content: Left early morning from Jibhi to Aut. Boarded bus/train back to Delhi. Long journey, but didn’t feel like leaving.

tips: Talk to locals — they are genuinely helpful and kind.
Stay in homestays for better experience than hotels.
Carry cash — ATMs are unreliable in Jibhi.
Start Jalori hikes early for better views and less crowd.
Evenings get cold even in summer — carry warm clothes.
Avoid monsoon season due to landslides.

budget.note: Per person for 3–4 days (Delhi to Jibhi round trip, stay, food, and local travel).

includes: Stay, food, local travel, basic expenses

title: The Forest That Doesn't Need You to Be Brave
subtitle: India's second-largest tiger reserve — and the most undervisited national park I've ever loved.
shortDesc: Sal forests, wild elephants, and the sound of nothing. Simlipal is the trip that resets everything.
founderNote: Most people plan a wildlife trip hoping to see a tiger. In Simlipal I saw a mother elephant and her calf crossing a road at dusk, and I completely forgot tigers existed. That's what this forest does to you.
story: Simlipal is one of those places that doesn't photograph well. The jungle is too dense, the light too patchy, the mornings too misty. You can't capture it, so you just have to be there.

I'd been to Corbett, Ranthambore, Bandhavgarh — the famous parks. Simlipal is different. No tourist infrastructure, no tiger show, no jeep traffic jams. Just you and a forest ranger on a trail where elephants walked last night.

The sal trees here are enormous — some older than 200 years. The air smells of damp earth and something older than anything I can name. I've taken three groups here. Everyone comes back changed. None of them can explain exactly why.

days.title: Arrive at Baripada, enter the forest
days.content: We reach Baripada by train and drive the 60km to the Pithabata gate. Check into the forest rest house — basic, beautiful, surrounded by sal. Evening walk with the ranger to a waterhole. We heard elephants before we saw them.

days.title: Morning game drive, afternoon forest walk
days.content: A 4am start for the morning drive — this is when you see the most. Herds of gaur (Indian bison), spotted deer, and if you're quiet and lucky, a glimpse of stripes between the trees. Afternoon is a slow forest walk to the Bareipani falls.

days.title: Joranda Falls and the deep forest trail
days.content: The 150-metre Joranda waterfall is one of India's tallest, and almost no one knows it exists. The trail through the forest to reach it is the most beautiful three hours I've spent anywhere in India.

days.title: Leave slower than you arrived
days.content: No early starts on the last day. Breakfast on the veranda. A final walk. The drive back to Baripada always feels like re-entering a different world. It takes a day or two before normal life feels normal again.

tips: Simlipal is open November to June. The best wildlife sightings are February–April.
Entry permits are required — we handle all bookings as part of the trip.
No mobile network inside the reserve. Disconnect completely.
Wear muted colours (olive, brown, khaki) — bright clothes disturb wildlife.
Leeches in the wet season — tuck your socks over your trousers if visiting just after monsoon.

budget.note: Per person for 3 nights, all-inclusive from Kolkata (train + forest permits + accommodation + meals).

title: India's Best Sunrise That No One Is Watching
subtitle: The Queen of Chota Nagpur — and maybe the most peaceful plateau in the country.
shortDesc: Pine forests, red laterite soil, and a sunrise so vivid it looks like someone spilled paint across the sky.
founderNote: I've watched the Netarhat sunrise seven times. The first time I gasped. The seventh time I cried. It never stops being extraordinary. I bring people here because words genuinely cannot prepare you for it.
story: Netarhat sits at 3,700 feet above the Chota Nagpur plateau, three hours from Ranchi through forests that thin out into tall pines as you climb. The air changes somewhere around the second hour. You can feel when you've arrived even before you see anything.

The plateau is named after the Neta flowers that carpet it in spring. The British built a school here in 1924 — Netarhat Residential School — which still sits under the pines, giving the whole place an unlikely feeling of quiet academic calm.

The sunrise point is a 20-minute walk from the main road. You get there before dawn and wait. The Jharkhand sky does something extraordinary in those minutes before light — it cycles through violet, then blood orange, then gold, and when the sun finally clears the horizon over the plains below, it looks like the whole valley is on fire.

days.title: Reach Ranchi, drive up at dusk
days.content: The 3-hour drive from Ranchi to Netarhat is best done in the evening so you arrive in the dark and experience the reveal in the morning. Dinner at the rest house — simple Jharkhand food. Sleep early.

days.title: The sunrise. Everything else is secondary.
days.content: 5am start. Walk to the sunrise point. Watch in silence. Breakfast after. The rest of the day is forest walks, the old British-era bungalows, the Magnolia Point viewpoint, and a long lunch. Afternoon nap is non-negotiable.

days.title: Lower Ghaghari Falls and the slow drive home
days.content: An hour's drive to Lower Ghaghari falls — 320 metres, a cascade through dense forest. Swim if you're brave enough. The water is cold even in summer. Drive back to Ranchi through a different route. Stop when something looks interesting.

tips: October–March is the best time. Avoid summer (April–June) — can get hot and hazy.
The sunrise point gets cool quickly — carry a shawl or light jacket even in November.
Netarhat has no restaurants worth mentioning — the rest house meals are genuinely the best food option.
The road from Latehar to Netarhat has patches of poor connectivity — download offline maps.
Combine with Betla National Park (1.5 hrs away) if you have an extra day.

budget.note: Per person for 2 nights, all-inclusive from Ranchi (local transport + accommodation + meals).

title: The Place Where I Forgot About Time
subtitle: Days that started with mountain views and ended in quiet cafés — no rush, no plans, just living slowly.
shortDesc: A peaceful hill escape above McLeodganj where cafés, trails, and silence come together.
founderNote: I came here thinking I'd explore places. Instead, I ended up exploring a slower version of myself. Dharamkot doesn’t give you a checklist — it gives you space.
story: I reached McLeodganj expecting another busy hill station. Cafes, crowds, traffic — the usual. But someone told me to go 15 minutes uphill to Dharamkot. That decision changed the entire trip.

Dharamkot feels different. The noise drops, the air feels lighter, and suddenly you're not in a hurry anymore. I stayed in a small homestay overlooking the valley — nothing fancy, just clean rooms and a balcony that became my favorite place.

Most days had no plan. I’d wake up late, walk to a café, sit for hours with coffee, and just watch people come and go. Some were working, some were traveling, some just existed there. It didn’t feel like tourism — it felt like a pause.

One day I hiked to Triund. The trail wasn’t too hard, but the view at the top — snow peaks in front, clouds below — that stays with you. But honestly, the best moments weren’t the views. They were the quiet, in-between moments.

days.title: Arrive and move above the crowd
days.content: Reached McLeodganj by afternoon — crowded, noisy. Took a short cab ride up to Dharamkot. The difference was immediate. Checked into a small homestay, had simple dinner, and just sat outside doing nothing.

days.title: Cafés, slow walks, no agenda
days.content: Started late. Walked around Dharamkot, tried 2–3 cafés. No rush, no fixed plan. Just sitting, eating, and observing. Evening was cold, spent time with other travelers sharing stories.

days.title: Triund trek
days.content: Started early for Triund. The hike is steady but manageable. Reached the top in 3–4 hours. Sat there for a long time — no phone network, just mountains. Came back by evening, tired but clear-headed.

days.title: Hidden trails and waterfall
days.content: Explored random trails around Bhagsu. Found a small waterfall spot — not crowded. Spent time there, no hurry to return. Evening café again, same slow rhythm.

days.title: Leave, but not really
days.content: Packed slowly. Didn’t feel like leaving. Dharamkot has a way of making you want to stay longer. Took a cab back down, but mentally I was still up there.

tips: Stay in Dharamkot instead of McLeodganj for a peaceful experience.
Carry a light jacket — evenings get cold even in summer.
Network is weak in some areas (Jio works better than Airtel).
Start Triund trek early to avoid crowd and heat.
Cash is useful — many small cafés don’t accept cards.

budget.note: Per person for 5 days (budget travel with homestay, food, and local transport).

title: A Place I Keep Coming Back To
subtitle: Some trips end. Some places stay with you — and you return without thinking twice.
shortDesc: A mix of lively Darjeeling and peaceful Lamahatta — experienced over multiple visits.
founderNote: I’ve been here 4–5 times — and every time feels slightly different. Darjeeling is familiar, but Lamahatta is where I go when I want silence.
story: Darjeeling is one of those places that never feels new — yet never feels boring. I’ve been here 4–5 times, and every visit has been different.
The first time, I did all the usual things — Mall Road, cafés, viewpoints. But over time, I started slowing down, exploring less crowded corners.
That’s how I found Lamahatta. Just 40–45 minutes away, but completely different. No crowd, just pine trees, prayer flags, and silence.
Now whenever I come here, I split my time — a little bit of Darjeeling’s energy, and a little bit of Lamahatta’s calm.
It’s not about ticking places anymore — it’s about how the place makes you feel. And this one keeps pulling me back.

days.title: Kolkata → Siliguri → Darjeeling
days.content: Overnight train/bus from Kolkata to Siliguri (₹900 approx). From Siliguri, took NBSTC local bus to Darjeeling (₹105). Reached by afternoon. Evening walk at Mall Road.

days.title: Darjeeling local exploration
days.content: Visited local cafés, tea gardens, and viewpoints. No strict plan — just walked around and explored slowly. Evening again at Mall Road.

days.title: Lamahatta — the quiet side
days.content: Took a local ride to Lamahatta (40–45 mins). Completely peaceful — pine forests, prayer flags, and walking trails. Spent hours doing nothing.

days.title: Flexible / return
days.content: Optional sunrise (Tiger Hill) or free morning. Then return to Siliguri and back to Kolkata.

tips: Use NBSTC bus (₹105) — cheapest and reliable option.
Stay near Mall Road for easy access.
Visit Lamahatta early morning for best experience.
Carry light jacket — weather changes quickly.
Avoid peak crowd weekends if possible.
Must try: Momos, Thukpa, and Darjeeling tea at local cafés — simple places often taste better than fancy ones.
Underrated: Walk towards Lamahatta eco park trails or explore quiet roads beyond main market — less crowd, better views.

budget.note: Per person for 3–4 days (Kolkata to Darjeeling round trip + stay + food + local travel).

title: The Trek That Changed How I See Myself
subtitle: My first trek — where fear, nature, and clarity all came together.
shortDesc: From green valleys to snow and desert landscapes — a journey that pushes you physically and mentally.
founderNote: This was my first real step into the mountains. I went with fear — heights, unknown, everything. I came back different. Somewhere between those climbs and silence, I realized — you either love the mountains, or you don’t. There’s no middle.
story: Hampta Pass wasn’t just a trek for me — it was a turning point. My first proper trek, and honestly, I was scared before starting.
The first day started with green valleys — soft, open meadows, almost peaceful. But slowly, the terrain started changing.
From green to rocky brown, and then to snow. It felt like walking through different worlds in just a few days.
Crossing the pass was the hardest part — physically and mentally. But once you do it, something shifts inside you.
And then Chandratal. Completely different — cold, silent, and unreal. That moment just stays with you.
This trek made me realize something simple — mountains aren’t casual. Either you love them deeply, or you don’t connect at all.

days.title: Delhi → Manali
days.content: Overnight bus from Delhi to Manali (₹600 approx). Reached Manali by morning. Used Manali as a halt, not a destination.

days.title: Manali → Jobra → Trek starts
days.content: Reached basecamp (Jobra) via trek company vehicle. Started trek through forest trails and reached first campsite in green meadows.

days.title: Jwara to Balu Ka Ghera
days.content: Long walk through changing terrain — from green valleys to rocky landscapes. Campsite surrounded by mountains.

days.title: Hampta Pass crossing
days.content: The toughest day. Steep climb, snow patches, and finally crossing Hampta Pass. Descended to Spiti side — completely different landscape.

days.title: Chandratal Lake
days.content: Visited Chandratal — cold, silent, and unreal. One of the most beautiful places I’ve seen.

days.title: Return to Manali
days.content: Drive back to Manali. Rest day. Explored local market and nearby areas.

days.title: Sethan village exploration
days.content: Visited Sethan village — quiet, less crowded, and much better than busy Manali.

tips: Choose a good trek company — I went with Searching Souls (~₹8000 Manali to Manali) and had a great experience.
Indiahikes is premium and one of the best — but costs more.
Carry proper trekking shoes and warm layers — weather changes fast.
Your first trek will test you mentally more than physically.
Don’t treat Manali as a destination — it’s crowded and overhyped.
Must visit: Sethan village — peaceful and underrated compared to Manali.
Stay hydrated and follow your guide — safety matters a lot in this trek.

budget.note: Per person for full trek (Delhi to Delhi, including trek package, travel, and local exploration).

includes: Travel, trek package, food, stay, guide

title: The Trek Where I First Saw Real Snow
subtitle: Freezing nights, white landscapes, and lessons I didn’t expect to learn.
shortDesc: A winter trek with real snow experience — less crowded, more raw than popular routes.
founderNote: This was my first real snow experience. -15°C, frozen water, and nights that test you. But somewhere in that cold, I learned how to handle discomfort — and enjoy it.
story: Kuari Pass was my first time experiencing real snow — not just seeing it, but living in it.
Temperatures dropped to around -15°C. Water bottles froze. Hands went numb if you stayed still too long.
But once you start walking, something changes. The cold becomes part of the experience.
The trail was covered in snow — clean, silent, and raw. No noise, no distractions, just you and the mountains.
This trek taught me something simple — it’s not about carrying more, it’s about carrying smart. And understanding how to adapt.

days.title: Delhi → Rishikesh
days.content: Overnight bus from Delhi to Rishikesh (₹450 approx). Reached early morning and met trek group.

days.title: Rishikesh → Basecamp
days.content: Drive to basecamp (Joshimath side). Long but scenic journey through mountains.

days.title: Trek begins — snow starts
days.content: Started trek. First signs of snow on trail. Cold increased, but manageable while walking.

days.title: Deeper into snow zone
days.content: Trail fully covered in snow. Walking became slower. Campsite extremely cold at night.

days.title: Kuari Pass summit
days.content: Reached Kuari Pass. Clear views of Himalayan peaks. Cold wind, but unforgettable moment.

days.title: Return trek + basecamp
days.content: Descended back through same trail. Slightly easier but still tiring.

days.title: Return to Rishikesh → Delhi
days.content: Drive back to Rishikesh and overnight bus to Delhi.

tips: Carry proper thermal layers — layering matters more than heavy jackets.
Warmee or heat packs are very useful — almost lifesaving in extreme cold.
Pack light — don’t carry unnecessary items.
Good trekking shoes are a must (snow grip matters).
Stay hydrated even if you don’t feel thirsty.
Kuari Pass is a better choice than Kedarkantha if you want less crowd and more real experience.
Mentally prepare for cold — it’s not easy, but worth it.

budget.note: Per person for full trek (Delhi to Delhi including trek package and travel).

includes: Travel, trek package, stay, food, guide

title: Where I Finally Slowed Down
subtitle: A quiet village above Kasol where time moves slower and everything feels lighter.
shortDesc: Kalga offers peace, forests, and real mountain life — away from Kasol’s crowd.
founderNote: Kasol is crowded, but Kalga changed the whole experience for me. Staying there felt peaceful, simple, and real — the kind of place where you don’t feel like leaving.
story: Most people go to Kasol and stay there. I did too at first — but it felt crowded and noisy.
Then I moved to Kalga village, just a short distance away, and everything changed.
Kalga is quiet. No loud cafés, no rush — just wooden houses, apple trees, and mountain views.
I stayed there for 4 days, doing almost nothing — walking around, exploring nearby villages, and just relaxing.
Food was slightly expensive compared to cities, but worth it. One small homestay served chicken curry and rice — simple, but one of the best meals I had.
This place is not about doing things. It’s about slowing down and feeling the mountains.

days.title: Delhi → Kasol → Kalga
days.content: Overnight bus from Delhi to Kasol. From Kasol, took local transport to Barshaini and short hike/ride to Kalga village. Checked into homestay and relaxed.

days.title: Kalga village exploration
days.content: Walked around Kalga — explored forests, small trails, and nearby viewpoints. Completely peaceful day.

days.title: Kheerganga trek
days.content: Started early (7–8 AM) for Kheerganga trek (~12 km). Trail passes through forests, waterfalls, and small dhabas. Reached top by afternoon. Hot water spring experience.

days.title: Nearby villages — Tosh / Pulga
days.content: Explored nearby villages like Tosh and Pulga. Each has a different vibe — Tosh slightly lively, Pulga more quiet.

days.title: Return journey
days.content: Descended back to Barshaini → Kasol → overnight bus to Delhi.

tips: Stay in Kalga instead of Kasol for a peaceful experience.
Start Kheerganga trek early (7–8 AM) to avoid rush and heat.
Food is slightly expensive but worth it — quality is good.
Must try: Simple homestay meals (like chicken curry + rice) — very homely taste.
Carry cash — limited digital payment options.
Explore nearby villages: Tosh (lively) and Pulga (quiet, underrated).
Walk around forests in Kalga — best experience is unplanned exploration.

budget.note: Per person for 3–4 days (Delhi to Kasol round trip + stay + food + local travel).

title: Not for Everyone — But Unforgettable If It Connects
subtitle: A quiet Himalayan stop where silence, stars, and simplicity take over.
shortDesc: A peaceful village beyond Atal Tunnel with waterfalls, clean skies, and raw mountain vibe.
founderNote: Sissu is not for everyone — but if it connects, you’ll never forget it. Clean air, quiet nights, and a sky full of stars.
story: I stopped at Sissu after Manali — just a 2-day stay, but it felt completely different from the usual Himachal spots.
Crossing Atal Tunnel changes the landscape instantly. It feels raw, open, and less crowded.
Sissu is quiet. No chaos, no loud cafés — just mountains, clean air, and space.
The waterfall here is beautiful — and you can even reach it through a zipline, which makes the experience different.
But the best part was the night. Clear sky, no light pollution, and full stargazing. That’s something you don’t easily get in crowded places.
This place is not special for everyone — but for the right kind of traveler, it becomes unforgettable.

days.title: Delhi → Manali (halt)
days.content: Overnight bus from Delhi to Manali. Stayed for a short halt. Manali felt crowded and busy.

days.title: Manali → Atal Tunnel → Sissu
days.content: Crossed Atal Tunnel and reached Sissu (1–1.5 hrs). Landscape changes completely after the tunnel. Checked into stay and explored nearby areas.

days.title: Waterfall & stargazing
days.content: Visited Sissu waterfall — can also try zipline for a different experience. Evening and night spent stargazing under clear sky.

days.title: Return
days.content: Returned back via Atal Tunnel to Manali and continued journey back.

tips: Use Manali only as a halt — not the main stay.
Stay options are limited — book in advance.
Food is slightly expensive but worth it due to remote location.
Must try: Local Himachali food in small cafés or homestays.
Don’t miss stargazing — one of the best experiences here.
Visit Sissu waterfall and try zipline if available.
This place is best for people who enjoy silence and slow travel.

budget.note: Per person for 2–3 days (Delhi to Sissu round trip + stay + food + local travel).

title: My Go-To Place When I Want to Reset
subtitle: Cafés, conversations, and a place that always feels familiar no matter how many times I visit.
shortDesc: A mix of calm café life, river views, and global traveler vibes in the yoga capital of India.
founderNote: Rishikesh (Tapovan) is my default reset place. I’ve been here multiple times — not for sightseeing, but for the vibe, the people, and the feeling of just being.
story: Rishikesh is not just a place I visited — it’s a place I keep going back to.
Tapovan especially has a different energy. You walk around and easily 6 out of 10 people are foreigners. It doesn’t feel like a typical Indian town.
Café hopping became my routine here. Sitting for hours with coffee, looking at the mountains, watching people come and go.
What made it special for me was the people. I met travelers from Spain, Australia, Japan, Israel, Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan, Vietnam — and had real conversations.
You learn a lot from them — how they see travel, how they live, how differently they think.
Some of them even invited me to visit their countries — jokingly, I feel like I already have free stays booked worldwide now.
Evenings at the ghats are simple but powerful. Sitting quietly, sipping ginger lemon honey tea, watching the Ganga flow — that’s enough.

days.title: Delhi → Nepali farm →Rishikesh (Tapovan)
days.content: Bus from Delhi to Nepali farm (₹400–₹500) to Tapovan (share taxi 100/) . Reached Tapovan area and checked into hostel/homestay. Evening walk around cafés.

days.title: Café hopping & local exploration
days.content: Explored cafés like Moktan Café and small bakeries. Spent time working, relaxing, and meeting new people.

days.title: Activities & Ghat time
days.content: Optional activities like rafting, bungee jumping. Evening spent at ghats with tea and calm surroundings.

days.title: Flexible / slow day
days.content: No fixed plan — just café hopping, conversations, and enjoying the vibe.

tips: Stay in Tapovan area — best mix of calm and café culture.
Must try: Moktan Café and local bakeries.
Only veg food available — but variety is good.
Try ginger lemon honey tea at ghats — simple but perfect.
Café hopping is the main experience here.
Talk to fellow travelers — you’ll learn a lot.
Best place for solo travelers and slow travel vibe.

budget.note: Per person for 3–4 days (Delhi to Rishikesh round trip + stay + food + basic activities).

includes: Stay, food, local travel, basic activities
